00_thesis/meeting_notes/2026-02-24.html.description:
Attend and research relevant conferences in your field
Learn Web of Science, Scopus, and IEEE Xplore for finding quality papers
Use LLMs to summarize papers and quickly judge if they're worth reading
Identify target journals and conferences for publication
Find websites that rank top-tier CS conferences
Browse conference sessions and stalk key authors publishing in relevant tracks

00_thesis/thesis-log.html.title: thesis-log
00_thesis/thesis-log.html.description:
2026-02-28
2026-03-01
2026-03-02
2026-03-04
2026-03-05
https://lalith.in/2020/09/27/Low-Level-Advice-For-Systems-Research prepare your experiment infrastructure as early as possible goal of prototypes is to exercise a certain hypothesis automate experiment workflow as systems get complex, ensure correctness with tests avoid tunnel vision. write first few paragraphs of paper's evaluation section Compare results of experiments from the system's same version use feature flags check features individually
 John Ousterhout: “Use your intuition to ask questions, not answer them”. Log extensively. Recommended reads:
“Performance Evaluation of Computer and Communication Systems”, Jean-Yves Le Boudec.
“The Art of Computer Systems Performance Analysis: Techniques for Experimental Design, Measurement, Simulation, and Modeling”, Raj Jain.
“Mathematical foundations of computer networking”, Srinivasan Keshav.
“Systems Benchmarking Crimes”, Gernot Heiser
 “Systems Benchmarking Crimes”, Gernot Heiser performance should improve significantly in the area of interest performance should not significantly degrade elsewhere demonstrate acceptability of new overheads pick experiments justly don't downplay/sugarcoat overheads show other stats, use geometric means split train and test (calibration and eval. it is called) consider baseline performance carefully when running other app be sure it doesn't have debugging and stuff like that enabled. Be sure that current app is the best working version of this baseline artifact document what you are doing I have been thinking about keeping a log of my thoughts and progress on thesis. This documentation will also help creating a report. Throughout the research, artifact/deliverable oriented task setting approach should be embraced.
https://obsidian.md/plugins?id=webpage-html-export Will use this extension to export my logs and thesis related files into a html file easing out the research tracking process. As this whole 00_thesis folder can be exported. I am considering adding some dashboards in the future. I can even transfer my blog to this setup. It is really handy!
Created /thesis route on my website. It is a password-protected private obsidian folder exported in html. First ever password of this route is distributedftw!123. For debugging, I have exposed this password; therefore, it will be changed. Environment setup is important for the future of the research. This log, for example, captures my flow of thoughts throughout the research process which will help me -- and the advisor -- track ideas. Next, i will be focusing on the following: seamless synchronization of /thesis and my local workspace developing a systematic approach to domain exploration and problem selection process
single source of truth for task management (jira, obsidian, github issues) #discuss I will place web export to a local folder and keep it synchronized to git:/src/private/thesis using a tool like rsync. #!/bin/bash #push_notes_to_web.sh OBSIDIAN_EXPORT="/home/matin/Documents/iztech/masters/thesis/web_notes" REPO_THESIS="/home/matin/Documents/github/matinhuseynzade.com/src/private/thesis" REPO="/home/matin/Documents/github/matinhuseynzade.com" # 1. Check if source is empty if [ -z "$(ls -A "$OBSIDIAN_EXPORT")" ]; then echo "Source is empty, nothing to do." exit 0 fi # 2. Sync to repo rsync -av --delete --exclude='index.html' "$OBSIDIAN_EXPORT/" "$REPO_THESIS/" # 3. Clean source folder rm -rf "$OBSIDIAN_EXPORT"/* # 4. Git push cd "$REPO" git add src/private/thesis/ git commit -m "chore: sync thesis $(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')" || echo "Nothing to commit" git push Exporting web output to OBSIDIAN_EXPORT and running push_notes_to_web.sh once a while will help me keep this page up-to-date.
Wondering if I should keep every day's note as a separate .md file like 2026-03-05.md? #meta
As for reading through papers, I think scientific method oriented approach should be embraced. Papers can be reduced into its simple phases which abides by the method: problem/question, hypothesis, experiment, result. #discuss

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
